questions.stem: Slide 5 frames the public-key distribution problem: Alice wants Bob's public key, but Mallory can substitute her own. What is the *circular* aspect that makes signing alone insufficient?

choices.text: Signing requires a hash that hasn't been computed yet.
If Bob signs his public key, you need his public key to verify the signature — the very thing you were trying to authenticate.
RSA signatures are not collision-resistant.
Mallory can break RSA with a quantum computer.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Verification requires the public key. If you don't trust the public key, the signature it produces is meaningless. You must bootstrap trust from somewhere else — a trust anchor.

distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Collision resistance isn't the issue.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Era** — Quantum is unrelated to the bootstrap problem.

questions.stem: Slide 7 says: "You cannot gain trust if you trust nothing. You need a root of trust." Mechanistically, what does a trust anchor enable?

choices.text: It is the entity whose public key is implicitly trusted (e.g., baked into the OS), allowing trust to be transitively extended to other parties via signed certificates.
It is a special algorithm that proves all keys are trustworthy.
It is a one-time pad that bootstraps the system.
It is a hash of all public keys ever issued.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Trust anchors are the bedrock: their public keys are pre-distributed (in OS/browser keystores). All other trust is derived from signatures chained back to a trust anchor.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Vague** — There is no algorithm that magically establishes trust.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Concept** — OTPs are for confidentiality.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Mechanism** — A global hash isn't the trust anchor model.

questions.stem: SSH uses Trust-on-First-Use (TOFU). What is the precise security assumption underlying TOFU?

choices.text: TOFU assumes attackers cannot perform any MITM.
TOFU assumes the *first* connection is unattacked; subsequent connections are protected by warning if the public key changes.
TOFU assumes the user verifies the key fingerprint out of band.
TOFU is equivalent to trusted-CA validation.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: TOFU's bet is that the first connection happens before the attacker is in position. Once the public key is pinned, any later substitution by Mallory triggers a warning — the user notices and refuses to proceed.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Overstatement** — TOFU is vulnerable on the *first* connection.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Stronger than TOFU** — Out-of-band verification is recommended but not required by TOFU.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — CA-based PKI requires no first-use leap of faith.

questions.stem: A certificate is described as a "signed endorsement of someone's public key." What two pieces of information must a certificate contain at minimum?

choices.text: The signer's secret key and the holder's public key.
The identity of the holder and the holder's public key, signed by the issuing authority.
Two random nonces.
An IP address and a port.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Certificates bind an identity to a public key via a third party's signature. Without the identity, you don't know whose key it is; without the key, there's nothing to authorize. The signature lets verifiers check the binding using the issuer's public key.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Catastrophic** — Secret keys must never appear in certificates.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Concept** — Nonces are for replay protection, not identity binding.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Layer** — Network endpoints aren't part of basic certificate semantics.

questions.stem: Slide 11 critiques the Trusted Directory model. What are the *two* main problems?

choices.text: Slow brute-force attacks and weak passwords.
Scalability (one directory cannot serve the world) and single point of failure (compromise of the TD breaks all trust).
Quantum computing and key length.
MITM and replay attacks.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: A single trusted directory is both an operational bottleneck (every key request hits one server) and a catastrophic single point of failure (its compromise undermines every certificate it has issued). CAs solve both via hierarchical delegation and multiple roots.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Topic** — That's password hashing, not directory architecture.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Anachronism** — Quantum is a separate concern, not the TD critique.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Layer** — These are protocol attacks, not architectural critiques.

questions.stem: Hierarchical CAs let a root CA delegate signing authority to intermediate CAs. What is the chief operational benefit?

choices.text: It makes the root CA's private key easier to share.
It scales the system: the root CA signs only a small number of intermediate CA certificates and can be kept offline; intermediates handle the high-volume work.
It eliminates the need for revocation.
It guarantees the root CA can never be compromised.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Root CA private keys are extraordinarily sensitive. Hierarchical delegation lets the root sign rarely (just intermediate CA certs) and stay offline most of the time, drastically reducing exposure. Intermediates do high-volume signing.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Catastrophic** — Sharing private keys is exactly what hierarchy avoids.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Revocation remains needed.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — No system guarantees uncompromisability.

questions.stem: Slide 14 explains certificate expiration. What is the design *paradox* LetsEncrypt resolved by choosing very *frequent* renewal?

choices.text: Frequent renewal is theoretically less secure but easier.
Frequent renewal forces automation, which is more reliable than rare manual renewal that gets forgotten and breaks websites.
Frequent renewal eliminates the need for revocation lists.
Frequent renewal makes certificates last longer.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Naively, rarer renewal seems more usable. But humans forget rare tasks. Forcing 90-day renewal makes manual processes infeasible, pushing operators toward automation — which is more reliable in practice.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Inverted** — Frequent renewal is *more* secure (smaller blast radius for compromised certs).
distractorAnalysis.C: **Overstatement** — CRLs still serve real purposes.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Logical Contradiction** — Frequent renewal means shorter validity per cert.

questions.stem: Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) are signed by the CA. What failure mode does Slide 15 highlight regarding CA availability?

choices.text: If the CA is unreachable, you must fail open and trust everything.
If the CA is unavailable, choosing 'fail-safe = reject everything' is too brittle (denial-of-service risk), but using a stale CRL might miss recent revocations — there is no clean answer.
CRLs cannot be signed.
CRLs require quantum-resistant signatures.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: CRL availability is a hard tradeoff: strict failure on CA unreachability lets attackers DoS the CA to break trust globally; lenient failure (use cached CRL) risks accepting recently revoked certs. Real systems compromise (e.g., OCSP stapling, short-lived certs).

distractorAnalysis.A: **Half-Truth** — Failing open is one option, but the slide presents it as a tradeoff, not a default.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — The slide explicitly says CRLs are signed.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Anachronism** — Quantum resistance is unrelated.

questions.stem: Slide 19 says "encrypt every user's password" is a bad idea. Why?

choices.text: Encryption is too slow for password verification.
An attacker who steals the password file likely also has access to the encryption key (stored on the same server), allowing decryption of all passwords.
Encrypted passwords cannot be verified.
Encryption requires a public key infrastructure.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Symmetric keys live with the system that uses them. A breach that exfiltrates the password file likely exfiltrates the key too. Hashing avoids this because there's no key to steal — only one-way digests are stored.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Reason** — Speed isn't the issue; key exposure is.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Decrypting and comparing works mechanically (it's just unsafe).
distractorAnalysis.D: **Confused** — Symmetric encryption is what's typically considered here.

questions.stem: What two hash properties are essential for password storage?

choices.text: Determinism (same password → same hash, enabling verification) and one-wayness (attacker cannot reverse hashes into passwords).
Reversibility and randomness.
Length extension and second-preimage resistance.
Speed and key dependence.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Determinism allows verification (re-hash and compare); one-wayness ensures stolen hashes don't immediately reveal passwords. Collision resistance is helpful but not strictly essential here.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Properties** — Length extension is a vulnerability, not a goal; second-preimage isn't the chief concern here.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Inverted** — Slow hashes are *desirable* for password storage.

questions.stem: Salts add per-user randomness to password hashes: $H(\text{password} \| \text{salt})$. What attack do salts specifically defeat?

choices.text: Brute-force attacks of any kind.
Pre-computation attacks (rainbow tables and dictionary attacks shared across users): with unique salts, the attacker must redo the work for every user instead of looking up a precomputed hash.
Timing attacks on hash comparison.
Network-level eavesdropping.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Without salt, $H(\text{password})$ is the same across all users with the same password — and attackers can pre-compute hashes for common passwords once and look them up. Per-user salt forces re-computation per user, multiplying the attack cost from $O(M+N)$ to $O(M \cdot N)$.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Overstatement** — Salts don't make brute force impossible; they multiply the work.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Defense** — Constant-time comparison defeats timing attacks; salts don't.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Layer** — Network attacks need TLS, not salting.

questions.stem: Slide 22 quantifies the attack cost: unsalted hashing costs $O(M + N)$ but salted hashing costs $O(M \cdot N)$, where $M$ is possible passwords and $N$ is users. Why?

choices.text: Salts double the hash size.

Without salt, the attacker hashes $M$ candidates once and then looks up $N$ users in $O(N)$. With per-user salts, the attacker must hash all $M$ candidates *for each* user's salt, giving $M$ work per user, $N$ users total.
Salts make hash functions slower per call.
Salts encrypt the user list.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The asymptotic flip is the central salt insight: unsalted lets the attacker amortize work across users; salted forces per-user attack budgets, eliminating the economy of scale.

distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Salts don't slow individual hash calls; the slow-hash decision is independent.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Concept** — Salts don't encrypt anything.

questions.stem: Slow hashes like PBKDF2 are designed to take orders of magnitude longer than SHA-256. What is the asymmetry that makes this acceptable?

choices.text: Slow hashes don't slow down attackers; only legitimate users notice.

Legitimate users compute the hash once per login (negligible cost), but an attacker brute-forcing millions of guesses pays the slow cost millions of times — multiplying their attack cost by the same factor.
Slow hashes are quantum-resistant.
Slow hashes have no collisions.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: A 100ms hash is unnoticeable to a single user typing a password but devastating to an attacker who must compute $10^9$ guesses. The asymmetry between one cost and many costs is the entire point of slow hashes.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Inverted** — Slow hashes specifically slow attackers; the user impact is incidental.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Property** — Slow hashes aren't a quantum defense.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — All hash functions have collisions (pigeonhole).

questions.stem: Slide 24 mentions Scrypt and Argon2 as alternatives to PBKDF2. What additional property do they provide?

choices.text: Faster verification on the server side.
High memory consumption per hash, defeating GPU/ASIC attackers who can parallelize many low-memory hashes but cannot scale memory linearly.
Hashes that are quantum-secure by construction.
Built-in encryption of the password.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: PBKDF2 is CPU-bound but memory-light; GPUs can compute thousands of PBKDF2 hashes in parallel. Memory-hard hashes (Scrypt, Argon2) require large memory per hash, dramatically reducing GPU/ASIC parallelism advantages.

distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Quantum resistance is a different concern.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Concept** — They hash, they don't encrypt.

questions.stem: Slide 25 distinguishes online vs offline password attacks. What is the precise difference?

choices.text: Online attacks use the internet; offline attacks don't.
Online attacks force the attacker to interact with the server (rate-limitable); offline attacks let the attacker compute hashes locally on stolen data, bypassing rate limits and parallelizing freely.
Online attacks are slower than offline attacks.
Online attacks always succeed; offline attacks always fail.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The key difference is who controls the rate: server (online — can rate-limit and lock accounts) vs attacker (offline — only bound by their hardware). Defenses differ accordingly: rate limits for online, slow+salted hashes for offline.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Distinction** — Both can use networks; the distinction is interaction with the verifier.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Inverted** — Offline attacks are typically much faster (no rate limit).
distractorAnalysis.D: **Absolutist Trap** — Both can succeed or fail.

questions.stem: If your password file has been exfiltrated by an attacker, what defense matters most against the resulting offline attack?

choices.text: Rate limiting on the login endpoint.
Strong password hygiene (long, random passwords) combined with salted, slow hashes — these are the only defenses that meaningfully slow an offline attacker.
Adding a CAPTCHA to the login form.
Disabling JavaScript on the login page.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Once the file is exfiltrated, the attacker is offline — server-side mitigations (rate limits, CAPTCHA) don't apply. The only protection is making each guess expensive (slow + salted) and the password space large (entropy in the password itself).

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Phase** — Rate limits help against online attacks only.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Phase** — CAPTCHAs are server-mediated.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Irrelevant** — JavaScript settings don't affect offline cracking.

questions.stem: Which threat does salting passwords NOT meaningfully defend against?

choices.text: Pre-computed rainbow tables shared across users.
An attacker brute-forcing a single targeted user's salted hash with effectively unlimited compute.
Detecting which two users share the same password.
Reusing precomputed dictionary attacks across many leaked databases.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Salting prevents amortization across users and reuse of precomputed tables. But it does not stop a determined attacker from grinding through the password space *for a single target* — the per-attempt cost is unchanged. Slow hashes are needed for that.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Defended** — With per-user salts, identical passwords produce different hashes.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Defended** — Salts vary per user and per database, so cross-database reuse fails.

questions.stem: Tying together certificate authorities and revocation, what are the *three* defensive layers a modern PKI uses to limit damage from a mis-issued certificate?

choices.text: Encryption, hashing, and password storage.
Short expiration dates (limit lifetime), revocation lists / OCSP (active invalidation), and Certificate Transparency logs (publicly auditable record of all issued certs).
Quantum resistance, post-quantum signatures, and longer keys.
Symmetric backup keys, MACs, and timestamps.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Defense-in-depth for PKI: (1) bound the time window with expiration; (2) actively invalidate via CRLs/OCSP when a cert is known compromised; (3) make all issued certs publicly auditable so mis-issuance is detectable. The lecture covers (1) and (2) explicitly; (3) is the modern extension.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Concern** — Quantum is orthogonal to mis-issuance defenses.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Confused** — Symmetric backups are not a PKI mechanism.

questions.stem: Combining salts, slow hashes, and online/offline attack defenses, which password storage scheme would the lecture endorse for a new authentication system in 2026?

choices.text: Store $\text{SHA-256}(\text{password})$.
Store $(\text{salt}, \text{Argon2id}(\text{password}, \text{salt}))$ with high memory and iteration parameters; rate-limit login attempts on the server side.
Store $\text{AES}(\text{key}, \text{password})$ with a server-side key.
Store $\text{HMAC}(K, \text{password})$ without salts.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Argon2id is the modern memory-hard slow hash; per-user salt blocks pre-computation; rate limits handle the online attack vector. This is the layered defense Slides 22–25 build toward.

distractorAnalysis.C: **Bad** — Encrypting passwords introduces the key-exposure problem.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Better than A but Inadequate** — HMAC isn't slow; lacks salt; key still vulnerable.

questions.stem: Approximately how many root CAs are pre-installed in modern operating systems and browsers, according to Slide 12?

choices.text: Around 5
Around 150
Around 10,000
Exactly 1

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The slide cites ~150 root CAs implicitly trusted by most devices. This number is significant — any one of them being compromised can mis-issue certificates for any domain, which is why monitoring (Certificate Transparency) matters.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Too few** — A handful would be operationally infeasible.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Too many** — That would be impossible to vet.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Trusted Directory model** — That's the failed single-TD design from Slide 11.
